_brief: Build a customer portal where enterprise clients can log in, view their invoices, download usage reports, and open support tickets. It needs to integrate with our existing Salesforce CRM and Stripe billing system. The team is 2 frontend engineers, 1 backend engineer, and 1 designer. We'd like to launch in Q2.
project_title: Enterprise Customer Portal with Salesforce & Stripe Integration
summary: A self-service portal for enterprise clients to manage invoices, download usage reports, and submit support tickets. Requires authenticated access with enterprise-grade security, bidirectional sync with Salesforce CRM for customer/ticket data, and Stripe integration for billing and invoice retrieval.

stories.title: Enterprise SSO integration (SAML/OIDC)
stories.notes: Enterprise clients typically require SSO; expect multiple IdP configurations

raid.description: Each enterprise client may have a different IdP — expect 2-3 weeks per client for SAML metadata exchange
Requires customer IT teams to provide SAML metadata — timeline outside your control

stories.title: Fallback email/password auth with MFA
stories.notes: Required for clients without SSO

raid.description: Assumes MFA via TOTP only — SMS/push auth adds 1-2 weeks and third-party dependency

stories.title: Role-based access control (admin vs. user)
stories.notes: Enterprise accounts need multi-user support with permissions

stories.title: Session management & security hardening
stories.notes: Token refresh, timeout policies, audit logging

stories.title: Stripe integration - fetch invoices by customer
stories.notes: Stripe API pagination, handling multiple subscriptions

raid.description: Multi-subscription customers may have complex invoice structures requiring custom pagination logic
Stripe sandbox environment required for testing — API rate limits differ from production

stories.title: Invoice detail view with line items
PDF invoice download
stories.notes: Stripe provides hosted URLs, but may need custom branding

stories.title: Payment status sync & display
stories.notes: Webhook handling for real-time status

stories.title: Define usage data model & source
stories.notes: Unclear where usage data lives - major scope variable

raid.description: Usage data source is undefined — if ETL is required this becomes a 4-6 week data engineering effort
Assumes usage data exists in a queryable format — unconfirmed

stories.title: Usage aggregation service/ETL
stories.notes: If usage data needs transformation, this is significant backend work

raid.description: Unstructured usage data requiring transformation is a project within a project — scope this before committing

stories.title: Usage dashboard with date range filtering
Scheduled report delivery (email)
stories.notes: Nice-to-have but enterprise clients often expect this

stories.title: Salesforce Service Cloud integration - API setup
stories.notes: OAuth, connected app configuration, field mapping

raid.description: Requires Salesforce admin to configure Connected App and provide OAuth credentials — schedule week 1
Assumes standard Service Cloud Case object — custom objects or validation rules will require rework

stories.title: Ticket list view with status filters
Ticket detail view with comment thread
stories.notes: Bidirectional sync of comments is complex

raid.description: Bidirectional comment sync requires webhook or polling — Salesforce webhook reliability is inconsistent

stories.title: File attachment support
stories.notes: Storage and virus scanning considerations

stories.title: Real-time ticket status updates
stories.notes: Salesforce webhooks or polling strategy needed

raid.description: Salesforce outages will silently drop status updates unless retry and dead-letter queue are implemented
Depends on Salesforce webhook availability — no SLA guarantee on event delivery

stories.title: Customer data sync (account, contacts)
stories.notes: Need to map portal users to SF contacts

stories.title: Error handling & retry logic
stories.notes: SF API rate limits and downtime handling

stories.title: Design system/component library
stories.notes: If not reusing existing system

stories.title: Accessibility compliance (WCAG 2.1 AA)
stories.notes: Enterprise often requires this contractually

stories.title: Empty states, loading states, error states
Security review & pen testing
stories.notes: Enterprise customers will ask for SOC2, security questionnaires

raid.description: Enterprise clients may require SOC2 questionnaires or third-party pen test — add 2-3 weeks for remediation
Assumes no HIPAA or FedRAMP requirements — confirm with each enterprise client before sprint 1

stories.title: Integration test suite for SF/Stripe
stories.notes: Sandbox environments add friction

raid.description: Salesforce sandbox refresh cycles (typically weekly) can invalidate test data mid-sprint

stories.title: UAT with pilot customers
stories.notes: Calendar time, not effort

assumptions: SSO is required (enterprise standard) - adds 3-4 weeks vs. simple auth
Usage data source exists and is accessible via API - if not, add 4-6 weeks
Salesforce and Stripe sandbox environments are already configured
No major Salesforce customization required (standard objects)
Designer is dedicated to this project, not split across other work
Q2 means end of June (13 weeks from Jan 1, or ~18 weeks from mid-Feb)
No compliance requirements beyond standard security (no HIPAA, FedRAMP, etc.)

roles.notes: Adequate for portal UI, may bottleneck during integration testing
SEVERELY CONSTRAINED - Salesforce, Stripe, auth, usage reports all backend-heavy
Adequate if no other projects; needs to front-load work
NOT ALLOCATED - who is testing integrations?
NOT ALLOCATED - who owns infra and security review?
NOT ALLOCATED - who is making scope decisions and coordinating UAT?

red_flags: Single backend engineer is a critical-path bottleneck for a project with two major third-party integrations
No dedicated QA risks shipping bugs to enterprise customers who have low tolerance for issues
No PM/TPM allocated - scope creep and coordination overhead will fall on engineers
If backend engineer is unavailable (PTO, sick, attrition), project stops completely

risks.risk: Salesforce integration complexity - custom objects, validation rules, or triggers in your SF org can break assumptions and require rework
risks.mitigation: Conduct SF schema review in week 1; get SF admin involved; build integration spike before committing to timeline

risks.risk: Stripe multi-entity complexity - if customers span multiple Stripe accounts or have complex subscription structures, invoice retrieval becomes significantly harder
risks.mitigation: Audit Stripe data model upfront; confirm 1:1 customer-to-Stripe mapping

risks.risk: SSO configuration delays - enterprise IdP setup (Okta, Azure AD, etc.) requires customer IT involvement and often takes 2-4 weeks per customer
risks.mitigation: Start SSO conversations with pilot customers immediately; have fallback auth ready for launch

risks.risk: Usage data source undefined - if usage data doesn't exist in a queryable format, this becomes a data engineering project
risks.mitigation: Clarify usage data source in week 1; if it requires ETL, consider descoping from v1

risks.risk: Backend engineer burnout or departure - single point of failure for all integration and API work
risks.mitigation: Add 0.5-1.0 FTE backend support; cross-train frontend engineers on backend basics

risks.risk: Enterprise security requirements emerge late - SOC2 questionnaires, pen test findings, or data residency requirements can force rework
risks.mitigation: Engage security team now; get ahead of customer security reviews; budget 2 weeks for remediation

risks.risk: Q2 deadline is not achievable with current scope and team
risks.mitigation: Either reduce scope (cut SSO, cut usage reports from v1) or add backend headcount immediately

confidence.rationale: The brief has multiple undefined elements (usage data source, SSO requirements, Salesforce customization level) and the team is undersized for the integration complexity. Two major third-party integrations with a single backend engineer is a known failure pattern. Q2 launch is aggressive.
what_would_increase_confidence: Confirm exact usage data source and access method
Audit Salesforce org for custom objects and existing integrations
Clarify SSO requirements - is enterprise SSO required for launch or a fast-follow?
Add at least 0.5 FTE backend support
Define MVP scope explicitly - what can be cut if timeline slips?
Confirm Q2 means June 30 vs. April 1 (massive difference)

open_questions: Where does usage data live today, and what format is it in?
Is enterprise SSO (SAML/OIDC) required for launch, or can we ship with email/password first?
How customized is your Salesforce org? Any managed packages, custom objects, or validation rules on Cases?
Do customers have one Stripe customer ID each, or are there multi-subscription/multi-account scenarios?
What does 'Q2' mean specifically - April 1, June 30, or something in between?
Are there existing security/compliance requirements (SOC2, GDPR, specific customer DPAs)?
Who is the product owner making scope tradeoff decisions?
Is there an existing design system to reuse, or starting from scratch?
Are there pilot customers identified for UAT, and are they already engaged?

dimensions.rationale: Usage data source undefined — could add 4-6 weeks if ETL required
dimensions.action: Confirm where usage data lives and whether API or direct DB access exists

dimensions.rationale: No QA, no DevOps, single backend engineer for two major integrations
dimensions.action: Add 0.5 FTE backend support and dedicated QA for integration testing

dimensions.rationale: Salesforce org not audited; Stripe multi-subscription complexity unknown
dimensions.action: Run Salesforce schema audit and Stripe data model review in week 1

dimensions.rationale: 20-week realistic estimate is aggressive but achievable if no unknowns materialise
dimensions.action: Build in 2-week contingency buffer; identify which epics can be deferred to v2

dimensions.rationale: 3 of 7 assumptions unverified — usage data, Salesforce config, Q2 deadline definition
dimensions.action: Answer top 3 open questions before sprint planning begins
